marquee.experience: With 5+ years of UI/UX design experience
marquee.trading: Love creating and collaborating with clients to build brand IPs

deviceShowcase.label: WEB & APP PROJECTS
deviceShowcase.description: Product design experience across Fintech, Web3, and Crypto

exora.title: Exora — White-Label Perpetual DEX
exora.description: A derivatives trading platform integrating Hyperliquid liquidity, redesigning order placement and mobile experience — bringing CEX-level fluidity to DEX.

prediction-market.title: End-to-End AI Workflow Delivery: Spec, Design & Frontend — Solo
prediction-market.description: From Spec to frontend, building a Prediction Market PWA that users can easily use on mobile

hydra.title: Derivatives Trading Platform Integrating Hyperliquid Liquidity
hydra.description: Combining DeFi liquidity with CEX-like trading experience through multi-tenant architecture and shared liquidity.

cex-web.title: Modular CEX Homepage Design with Multiple Possibilities

web3vid.description: A Web3 all-in-one video platform for crypto creators — reached Hong Kong Digital Programme finals

rolling-x.title: Rolling X

hero.label: AAPD UI BOOTCAMP — GOLD AWARD
hero.title: An AI Tutor Built for Learning
hero.subtitle: A companion platform for exploring AI courses

overview.label: 01 — OVERVIEW
overview.heading: A project at the tipping point of AI education
overview.body:
In July 2023, I joined the AAPD UI Bootcamp — an intensive program focused on elevating UI design and UX decision-making skills. Over three weeks, I worked with four designers from different industries to complete this project, winning the Gold Award among 200+ participants.

The timing was critical: ChatGPT had launched just six months prior, and Duolingo along with other global platforms released their AI features in 2023. We chose an AI course learning platform not to chase trends, but based on our judgment that AI-assisted learning was transitioning from concept to product.
overview.calloutTitle: The market later validated this judgment
overview.calloutBody: The global AI education market is projected to grow from $5.88B in 2024 to $32.27B by 2030 (CAGR 31.2%). Khan Academy's AI tutor Khanmigo grew from 68K users to over 1.4 million.
overview.calloutSource: — Grand View Research, 2025; Khan Academy Official Data
overview.imageCaption: Platform overview — Homepage, course detail, and AI tutor

myRole.label: 02 — MY ROLE
myRole.heading: Leading visual direction & engineering handoff
myRole.intro: In two intense weeks, I was primarily responsible for two things:
myRole.card1Title: Led the overall product design direction
myRole.card1Body: From moodboard to final visual language, including color system, typography standards, and IP character style direction. The team aligned on my proposed direction before beginning individual interface design work.
myRole.card2Body: UI Flow connections, interaction state annotations for every screen, and component behavior specifications. This is a core competency from my previous work experience, enabling rapid integration within limited time.
myRole.collab: Other collaborative areas included competitive analysis, component design and iteration, and interface design — completed through team division of work.

research.label: 03 — RESEARCH
research.heading: From market observation to pain point synthesis
research.body: We observed AI courses proliferating across major learning platforms as a trending new category. Through interviewing AI learners and competitive analysis, we understood the product landscape and market, using the CIRCLES framework and 5W1H to define product positioning.
research.img1Caption: AI course category trends across platforms
research.fiveW1H_who1: AI beginners and cross-disciplinary learners (want to learn AI but don't know where to start)
research.fiveW1H_who2: Instructors across fields (want to share AI tool expertise)
research.fiveW1H_what1: An online learning platform focused on AI courses, with a 'site-wide companion AI tutor' as core differentiator
research.fiveW1H_when1: July 2023 — just 6 months after ChatGPT launched, AI education shifting from concept to product
research.fiveW1H_when2: AI courses booming across platforms, but no one had nailed the 'AI-assisted learning experience' itself
research.fiveW1H_where1: Browsing the homepage (don't know what to learn → AI helps find courses)
research.fiveW1H_where2: Hesitating on course pages (unsure if it's right → AI answers instantly)
research.fiveW1H_where3: During learning (stuck on a concept → AI acts as a tutor)
research.fiveW1H_why1: Three core pain points: can't find the right course, uncertainty before purchase, lack of companionship while learning
research.fiveW1H_how1: Floating Chat Panel (site-wide), with three contextual Tabs (Find Courses / Course Q&A / Learning Assistant) to reduce blank canvas cognitive load
research.pain1Body: Overwhelmed by the volume of AI courses with no clear starting point. Traditional category filters can't understand natural language needs.
research.pain2Body: Still uncertain after reading course descriptions — is the difficulty right for me? What prerequisites do I need? No immediate answers available.
research.pain3Body: When stuck on a concept, the only options are rewatching videos or searching on your own — missing a 'just ask and understand' tutor role.

goal.label: 04 — GOAL
goal.heading: Defining scope through User Stories
goal.story1: I want to browse the latest and trending courses on the homepage to understand what the platform offers
goal.story2: I want to view course details, instructor bio, and syllabus on the course page to judge if it's right for me
goal.story3: I want to quickly find courses matching my needs through search
goal.pos1Highlight: platform focused on exploring and learning AI
goal.pos2Before: Help users interested in AI who
goal.pos2Highlight: want to quickly master AI learning
goal.pos3Before: Bring together instructors across fields to share AI tool applications,
goal.pos3Highlight: fostering cross-disciplinary learning and innovative thinking
goal.pos4Before: Through AI tutor, provide users with a
goal.target: Target audience — Students: AI beginners, cross-discipline learners / Instructors: AI experts, educators across fields

keyDecision.label: 05 — KEY DECISION
keyDecision.heading: AI Tutor presentation: tradeoffs of two approaches
keyDecision.intro: The AI tutor is the product's biggest differentiator. When deciding its presentation form, we explored two approaches, each with clear tradeoffs.
keyDecision.optionACons:
Must leave the course page, interrupting learning flow
High page-switching cost
keyDecision.optionBRef: Persistent bottom-right icon → expand chat panel
keyDecision.optionBPros:
Available site-wide, doesn't interrupt current page
Users already have a mature mental model for this pattern
keyDecision.reason: Our AI tutor is positioned as a 'site-wide companion' — not just helping you learn in courses, but helping you find courses on the homepage and resolving purchase hesitations on course pages. The floating panel covers all User Stories simultaneously.
keyDecision.insightBody:
NN/g research indicates that 'conversation starters' are becoming a standard UI pattern in genAI tools, solving the 'blank canvas problem' — users not knowing what to ask. Our 'context tab' design is based on this insight.

The same research found that users tend to use more direct language with chatbots and heavily rely on preset buttons to reduce input cost.
keyDecision.insightSource: — Nielsen Norman Group, "Prompt Controls in GenAI Chatbots", 2025

deepDive.label: 06 — DESIGN DEEP DIVE
deepDive.heading: Context Tabs — reducing blank canvas cognitive load
deepDive.body:
After opening the chat panel, users face an empty conversation box. According to NN/g research, the core value of use-case prompt suggestions lies in learnability: helping users quickly understand what the AI tool can do.

Based on this insight, we designed three preset context tabs in the chat panel:
deepDive.tab1Desc: Describe your needs in natural language, AI recommends matching courses. More intuitive than traditional filter + keyword search.
deepDive.tab1Example: "I'm a designer, want to learn AI image generation"
deepDive.tab2Desc: While browsing course info, directly ask AI about difficulty, prerequisites, etc., reducing purchase decision uncertainty.
deepDive.tab3Desc: Concept clarification, learning progress suggestions, review highlights — letting AI play a true tutor role.
deepDive.tab3Example: "Summarize the three key points of this chapter"
deepDive.decision: Why tabs instead of pure open input? Context tabs balance two goals: reducing first-use cognitive load while preserving an open input field for advanced users. This design lets users unfamiliar with AI tools get started immediately, rather than facing a blank input box with no idea what to do.
deepDive.imageCaption: AI Tutor Panel — Preset context tabs + open input

iteration.label: 07 — ITERATION
iteration.heading: Restructuring information hierarchy to amplify product highlights
iteration.body: (I Led) After receiving mentor feedback, I re-proposed the homepage's visual hierarchy and information architecture. The core issue: the original design lacked product character and was indistinguishable from other learning platforms.
iteration.before1: Lacked product character, indistinguishable from competitors
iteration.before2: Course card component details could be improved
iteration.after1: Added IP character animation to strengthen brand identity
iteration.after3: AI tutor always present in bottom-right, ready anytime

finalDesign.label: 08 — FINAL DESIGN
finalDesign.heading: Complete Interface Showcase
finalDesign.body: Designed intuitive exploration and search functionality based on User Stories, enabling users to quickly find courses matching their needs.
finalDesign.img1Caption: Homepage — Explore search + trending courses + AI tutor entry
finalDesign.video1Caption: Prototype Demo — Homepage hero search interaction flow
finalDesign.img2Caption: Course Detail — Introduction, instructor bio, syllabus, reviews
finalDesign.img3Caption: AI Tutor Panel — Context tab guidance + conversational interaction
finalDesign.video2Caption: Prototype Demo — AI tutor chat panel interaction flow

handoff.label: 09 — HANDOFF
handoff.heading: UI Flow & Engineering Handoff
handoff.body: (I Led) Connecting User Stories to UI Flow with engineering annotations is a core skill I've long been responsible for in past work. Within the limited competition timeline, I rapidly integrated complete page transition logic, interaction state definitions, and component behavior specs.
handoff.imageCaption: UI Flow — Complete page transition logic & engineering annotations

designSystem.label: 10 — DESIGN SYSTEM
designSystem.heading: Component State Design & Organization
designSystem.body: (Team Collab) I established the component state specifications and naming logic, while team members completed detailed design for each component.
designSystem.imageCaption: Design System — Component states & interaction specifications

validation.label: 11 — MARKET VALIDATION
validation.heading: Two years later, the market validated our judgment
validation.body: This project was completed in August 2023. Our prediction that 'AI tutors will become standard for learning platforms' was fully validated two years later.
validation.stat1Detail: Hahow surpassed 500K learners and introduced its AI learning assistant in 2024, pioneering AI integration in Taiwan's online learning
validation.stat2Detail: Global AI education market projected to grow at 31.2% CAGR from 2025-2030
validation.stat3Detail: 84% of US high school students use generative AI tools for learning in 2025

reflection.label: 11 — REFLECTION
reflection.heading: Learning Together, Achieving Together
reflection.seg1Accent: In this project, my role focused on product style proposals, interface design, and UI Flow with engineering handoff annotations.
reflection.seg1Normal: It was also my first time collaborating with multiple designers on a single project.
reflection.seg2Accent: During the early UX research and feature mapping phase,
reflection.seg3Accent: I found that I could quickly draw connections to relevant competitors, map out possible flows, and offer direction and input to teammates.
reflection.seg3Normal: Later, while organizing the Design System and building Prototypes, I discovered the strengths of my teammates —
reflection.seg4Accent: and went on to apply those methods in my own work projects afterward!
reflection.seg5Normal: Beyond unexpectedly discovering my own potential, I also learned how to divide work among multiple designers through collaboration. Because everyone had different strengths, we could help and learn from each other, while sharpening both my UX thinking and UI expertise. Truly a rewarding experience!
reflection.body2: In this project, I came to realize two things.
reflection.insight1Title: The value of quickly setting visual direction
reflection.insight1Body: In just two weeks, the team needed a clear design style anchor to work in parallel. By quickly completing the moodboard and visual proposal early on, four designers could progress independently under a unified design language, significantly reducing late-stage integration conflicts.
reflection.insight2Title: The importance of engineering handoff documents
reflection.insight2Body: UI Flow and engineering annotation deliverables became key to the team's success in this competition — judges evaluated not just beautiful screens, but also the completeness of the handoff.

hero.label: Trading Platform
hero.title:
Exora
White-Label Perpetual DEX
powered by Hyperliquid
hero.subtitle: Redesigning order placement and mobile experience on an on-chain derivatives trading product — bringing CEX-level fluidity to DEX.

highlights.metric1Label: Abstracted 6-layer fund architecture into 3 user-facing states
highlights.metric2Label: White-label brand switching without modifying any component structure
highlights.metric3Label: Research → UX Architecture → Visual Design → Design System → PWA

meta.scopeValue: Trading UX · Fund Flow · Design System

summary.label: Project Summary
summary.headline: Bringing CEX-level fluidity to on-chain derivatives trading
summary.body: Exora is a perpetual futures trading product built on Hyperliquid. Starting from competitive analysis and user research, I led the visual direction, complete trading flow design, and Mobile PWA experience, and collaborated with front-end engineers to build a dual-layer Token design system supporting multi-tenant brand switching.

context.label: 01 — PROJECT CONTEXT
context.heading: Market Landscape
context.body: CEX and DEX each have clear strengths and weaknesses. This persistent trade-off defines the current state of the crypto derivatives trading market.
context.userProblemCardBody: CEX is smooth but opaque; DEX is transparent but the experience is terrible. This either-or dilemma is a structural problem in the crypto trading market.
context.userProblemExtra: A real case from March 2026: a whale executed a ~$50M token swap on the Aave interface, and the trade was routed to a pool with only $73K in liquidity. Slippage hit 99%, and they recovered only ~$36K. The interface showed warnings, but the user confirmed the transaction on mobile — when warning design isn't intuitive enough, no amount of prompts can prevent a single misclick.
context.insightBody: Fluidity and transparency shouldn't be an either-or choice. The real design opportunity is: let users simultaneously get CEX operational efficiency and DEX asset transparency without needing to understand the underlying technology — and the way risk information is presented determines whether this experience is safe or dangerous.

userResearch.label: 03 — USER RESEARCH
userResearch.heading: Deep Analysis of Trading Experience Pain Points
userResearch.body: 4+ years of crypto trading experience enabled faster access to the core problems — not replacing the user perspective, but more precisely identifying which frictions truly affect trading decisions versus surface-level usability issues during research.
userResearch.proseQuote: I clearly wanted to use isolated margin for this position, but after placing the order I realized it was cross-margin 20x.
userResearch.proseContext: This is a real scenario encountered when trading on CEX — switching to a new trading pair, the system defaults to cross-margin 20x leverage. I thought I was using isolated margin to control risk, but my entire account balance was exposed as collateral in a single trade. This doesn't happen every time, but precisely because it's sporadic, users are more likely to overlook it —
userResearch.proseHighlight: the system's default value silently made a high-risk decision for the user, and the interface gave no reminder.
userResearch.proseTakeaway: This experience directly influenced the research focus — locking onto the core journey from "deciding to trade" to "trade completed," identifying critical friction points in the trading flow.
userResearch.trader1Desc: Needs simple onboarding, clear risk information, and foolproof mechanisms.
userResearch.trader2Desc: Extremely sensitive to speed and efficiency. Demands minimal steps, real-time feedback, and uninterrupted flow.
userResearch.trader3Desc: Relies on API stability and ultra-low latency.
userResearch.spectrumFooter:
Onboarding is a one-time barrier, but trading is the daily core experience.
Friction happens here. Retention is lost here.

painPoints.label: CEX PAIN POINT MAP
painPoints.heading: Three Core Experience Problems
painPoints.body: These aren't just "hard to use" — they're direct results of the platform's default logic and information architecture.
painPoints.card1Title: Silent Defaults for Leverage & Margin Mode
painPoints.card1Body: When switching to a new trading pair, the system defaults to cross-margin 20x leverage with no reminder that "this pair's margin mode is different from your last one." Users think they're using isolated margin to control risk, but their entire account balance is exposed as collateral in a single trade.
painPoints.card2Body: On CEX, liquidation price only appears in the positions panel after a position is established. At the moment of ordering, you don't know "at what price will this trade be force-liquidated." The platform provides a separate calculator tool, but it requires opening separately and manually entering parameters — not in the order flow.
painPoints.card3Title: Funding Rate Accumulated Costs Are Hidden
painPoints.card3Body: The funding rate doesn't show "how much funding rate has been deducted from this position so far" in the positions panel. Users think the trade "made 2%," but after deducting accumulated funding rates, net profit might be only 1.3% or even a loss.

researchConclusion.label: RESEARCH CONCLUSION
researchConclusion.heading: Three Designable High-Priority Pain Points
researchConclusion.body: Distinguishing "what design can solve" from "platform architecture/business model choices," focusing on the highest-impact intervention points.

competitive.label: 02 — COMPETITIVE ANALYSIS
competitive.heading: CEX as UX Baseline, DEX as Direct Competitor
competitive.body: Analyzed 7 exchanges (Binance, OKX, Kraken, Liquid, Hyperliquid, dYdX, Aster) to position Exora's differentiation across three dimensions.
competitive.tableTitle: Breaking the Impossible Triangle: From Trading Venue to Infrastructure Layer
competitive.tableFooter: Exora's opportunity lies in building a SaaS layer: making liquidity and matching engines shared resources, while using design to eliminate DeFi's experience friction.
competitive.insightBody: DEX competitors are all Trading Venues. Exora is a Trading Infrastructure Layer — enabling others to build their own Trading Venue.

designInsight.label: 04 — CORE DESIGN INSIGHT
designInsight.heading: Technical Architecture Is UX's Ceiling — and Its Foundation
designInsight.body: This project has two asymmetric design challenges — surface-level UX problems and deep architectural problems.
designInsight.layer1Body: Default value prominence, liquidation price upfront, funding rate visibility — these have clear design directions.
designInsight.layer2Body: Product architecture involves six-layer fund entities, Agent Key authorization mechanisms, cross-chain settlement — these are PM and engineering team decisions. The design challenge is: fully understand the constraints and boundaries of this system, then translate it into a streamlined experience where users only see three fund states and complete orders in one step.

tradingUX.label: 05 — DESIGN OUTPUT: TRADING UX
tradingUX.heading: Interface Design Principles Built for High-Pressure Trading Scenarios
tradingUX.body: Based on research pain points, established four design principles as the foundation for all trading interface decisions.

orderPanel.label: ORDER PLACEMENT
orderPanel.heading: Order Placement: Opening a Position
orderPanel.body: The highest design density segment of the entire trading experience — let thinking happen outside the interface, let execution happen inside the interface. Every design decision directly maps to a research pain point.
orderPanel.card1Title: Leverage & Margin Mode Prominently Marked → Pain Point A
orderPanel.card1Body: Inherited system default leverage multiplier and margin mode are presented in warning styles, using color and labels to clearly indicate "this value was not actively set this time."
orderPanel.card2Title: Estimated Liquidation Price Upfront → Pain Point B
orderPanel.card2Body: The Order Panel calculates and displays estimated liquidation price, margin requirements, fees, and slippage in real-time — all numbers update instantly as parameters are adjusted, showing risk boundaries before placing the order.
orderPanel.card3Title: Funding Rate Accumulation Visible → Pain Point C
orderPanel.card3Body: The positions panel directly shows "how much funding rate this position has accumulated so far," making hidden costs no longer invisible.
orderPanel.card4Body:
Traditional DEX: click order → wallet popup → manual confirm → wait for broadcast → filled.
Exora: click order → instantly filled ✓

onboarding.label: 06 — ONBOARDING EXPERIENCE
onboarding.heading: Eliminating Crypto's Technical Barriers
onboarding.body: Reshaping the deposit and login experience — replacing cumbersome wallet authorization with familiar Google login, and Tenant Vault advance mechanism completely eliminates the waiting feel of cross-chain settlement.

mobilePWA.label: 07 — MOBILE PWA
mobilePWA.heading: Bringing Futures Trading to Mobile
mobilePWA.body: Hyperliquid's mobile is a shrunken copy of desktop. Exora's mobile uses Binance App and OKX App as UX benchmarks, achieving near-native app experience via PWA.
mobilePWA.caption2: Trade — Order Panel fixed bottom · Long-press to prevent mistouch

visualStyle.label: 08 — VISUAL STYLE
visualStyle.heading: A Dark System with Brand Color
visualStyle.body: DeFi derivatives products are almost universally black background with gray text — achieving visual differentiation in this space is itself a competitive advantage. The color system is divided into four semantic layers.
visualStyle.insightBody: There's a design red line between brand color and functional color: even if a partner's brand color happens to be green or red, Bullish / Bearish colors must have recognizable differences. Color confusion can lead to misjudging direction during high-pressure decisions — this is a safety issue, not an aesthetic one.
visualStyle.beforeCaption: Exora default brand color trading interface
visualStyle.afterCaption: Same interface with tenant brand color applied, demonstrating Token architecture effect

fundFlow.label: 09 — FUND FLOW UX ARCHITECTURE
fundFlow.heading: Don't Let Users See Architecture, Let Them See States
fundFlow.body: Exora's fund system involves six entities across different chains and permission levels. The design solution abstracts the six-layer architecture into three clear fund states.

designSystem.label: 10 — DESIGN SYSTEM
designSystem.heading: Building a Scalable Design Language
designSystem.body: Simultaneously meeting three goals: product consistency, multi-tenant white-label customization, and engineering teams building independently without deviating from design intent.

collaboration.label: 11 — CROSS-FUNCTIONAL COLLABORATION
collaboration.heading: AI-Driven Design Workflow
collaboration.body: Proactively introduced AI tool chains, enabling every design decision to simultaneously address the dual needs of tenants and end users.

impact.label: 12 — IMPACT & OUTCOMES
impact.heading: How Design Influenced Product Outcomes
impact.body: Quantifying design contributions from three dimensions: trading experience, Design System, and product strategy.
impact.metric1Label: Six-layer fund architecture / abstracted to three user states
impact.metric2Label: Core DS components / three major module coverage
impact.metric3Label: Edge cases / aligned during design phase
impact.metric4Label: Design exploration efficiency / AI Workflow improvement
tableRows: Six-layer entity architecture directly exposed to users
Default value prominently marked + warning color
Only visible after opening position, separate calculator needed
Order panel shows estimated liquidation price in real-time
Positions panel directly shows accumulated costs
dsTableRows: Dual-layer architecture (Global → Semantic), white-label brand switching requires no component structure changes
Semantic Token naming directly maps to CSS variables, eliminating translation loss
Bull/Bear colors won't be overridden by tenant brand colors — safety design, not aesthetic preference
Partners deploying their own exchange, from years to weeks
Independently completed user research → UX architecture → visual proposal → DS → Mobile PWA
Proactively introduced Claude + Cursor + v0 + Figma MCP, ~40% design exploration efficiency improvement

reflection.label: 13 — REFLECTION
reflection.heading: Good Infrastructure Design Means Making Your Own Work Invisible
reflection.body:
The most profound takeaway from the Exora project is that technical architecture is UX's ceiling — and its foundation.

Having 4+ years of trading experience as a designer, the biggest advantage in this project wasn't design skills per se, but a body-level understanding of the danger of "margin mode being silently decided by default values" and the real cost of "missing TP/SL integration on DEX" during fast-moving markets. These pain points were already real memories before design even began.

The Agent Key mechanism turned "placing an order" from a technical operation into a pure trading decision; the Vault advance mechanism made "deposit waiting" completely disappear from the user experience. If these two architectural decisions didn't exist, no UI could eliminate those frictions. A designer's most valuable contribution early in a product is participating in architectural decisions, not just designing interfaces.

The user's mental model is where design starts, not the feature list. CEX account architecture is designed from the perspective of "what products the platform needs to support." Exora's account architecture is designed from the perspective of "the user feels they have money, and they want to trade with it." This single perspective shift determines whether the system's complexity is transparent or exposed to the user.

hero.label: WEB3 · VIDEO PLATFORM
hero.title: A Web3 All-in-One Video Platform for Crypto Creators & Viewers
hero.subtitle: Hong Kong Cyberport Programme Finalist

meta.scopeValue: UI UX Design, Feature Planning, Logo Design, Pitch Deck Design

context.label: 01 — PROJECT CONTEXT
context.heading: Building a Video Platform Where Projects, KOLs & Web3 Users All Thrive
context.body:
The founding team were crypto content creators who experienced two core pain points firsthand — mainstream video platforms impose policy restrictions on Web3 content (throttling, banned promo links, takedown risks), directly impacting creator reach and revenue. Meanwhile, Web3 information is scattered across Twitter, Telegram, YouTube, and more, with inconsistent quality and no trusted aggregation hub.

After identifying these market pain points, they decided to build a Web3 all-in-one video platform, starting with the Chinese-speaking market.
context.img1Caption: Requirements workshop with the founding team

problemDefinition.label: 02 — COMPETITIVE POSITIONING
problemDefinition.heading: Competitive Analysis & Platform Positioning
problemDefinition.body: Analyzing competitors from the perspective of each user role to identify competitive advantages and define platform positioning.
problemDefinition.group1Card1Adv1: More professional, comprehensive Web3 video content aggregation
problemDefinition.group1Card1Adv2: Systematic categorization aligned with market trends
problemDefinition.calloutBody: Build a video platform where all three roles (creators, viewers, projects) gain value, differentiated through Web3-native mechanics (wallet tasks, points, airdrops).

marketResearch.label: 04 — MARKET RESEARCH
marketResearch.heading: Global Crypto Users Growing Rapidly — Chinese Market Scale Supports MVP
marketResearch.body: According to Crypto.com and Triple-A market reports, Chinese-speaking crypto users reached approximately 60 million by 2023. With favorable policies and Bitcoin halving/ETF narratives, crypto user growth in 2024 is expected to be explosive.
marketResearch.calloutCard1Body: Massive new user influx means onboarding must be beginner-friendly — cannot assume wallet literacy.
marketResearch.calloutCard2Body: Chinese market scale is sufficient to support a Chinese-first MVP strategy.
marketResearch.calloutCard3Body: Rapid market cycles mean content categorization and update speed are core experience, not add-on features.

marketOpportunity.label: 05 — MARKET OPPORTUNITY
marketOpportunity.heading: The Potential Value of Web3 Information & Video Platforms
marketOpportunity.body: From interviews with Web3 media professionals, I learned that market trends and hotspots update extremely fast — information distribution must be "fast, precise, and impactful." In this context, Web3 information distribution compensation shows exceptional cost-effectiveness, making Web3 video platforms a highly promising opportunity.

platformDecision.label: 06 — PLATFORM DECISION
platformDecision.heading: Balancing Development Resources & User Experience
platformDecision.body: Web3 is a fast-paced industry with tight development timelines. With limited resources, I evaluated three development directions across development cost, user experience, and wallet integration complexity.
platformDecision.optionAPros:
Low development cost, ideal for rapid MVP validation
Cross-platform, no App Store review restrictions
Users prefer connecting wallets on desktop — smoothest Web integration experience
platformDecision.optionBCons:
Long development cycle
Web3 content may be removed by App Store / Google Play
Wallet integration requires WalletConnect — added complexity
platformDecision.optionCCons:
High wallet integration complexity
Offline scenarios irrelevant for video platforms
Service Worker maintenance cost
platformDecision.reason: We chose Web-first. Beyond resource considerations, the core reason is that most users — due to security awareness — still prefer desktop for on-chain interactions (wallet connections, transaction signing). Web provides the smoothest wallet integration experience.

competitiveAnalysis.label: 07 — COMPETITIVE ANALYSIS
competitiveAnalysis.heading: Analyzing Web3 Competitors to Extract Design Insights
competitiveAnalysis.body: Analyzed five Web3 video platform competitors — Odysee, 3Speak, DLive, D.Tube, and REVO — focusing on three core dimensions to identify product entry opportunities for Web3Vid.
competitiveAnalysis.card1Body: Odysee supports uploading any file format (video, music, ebooks); 3Speak lets creators build their own token markets. But users ultimately care about "is there good content," not decentralized storage itself.
competitiveAnalysis.card1Highlight: decentralization as infrastructure, not selling point
competitiveAnalysis.card2Body: All five competitors bind their own tokens (LBC, Hive/Speak, Lemons, DTUB, R3VO), but tokens lack liquidity with few early users. DLive takes 25% commission; D.Tube uploads consume VP voting power — barriers too high.
competitiveAnalysis.card2Highlight: Start with points system to validate mechanics
competitiveAnalysis.card3Body: D.Tube uses IPFS with public data but no algorithmic recommendations; REVO combines video with NFTs but is overly complex. Web3 video platforms generally have rough UX and high onboarding barriers.

designPrinciples.label: 08 — DESIGN PRINCIPLES
designPrinciples.heading: Three Core Principles from Research to Design
designPrinciples.body: Synthesizing early research, I established three design principles to guide all subsequent UI/UX decisions. Each principle directly responds to problems discovered during the research phase.
designPrinciples.card1Body: Users' mental model for watching videos doesn't need to be relearned (aligned with YouTube). Web3 features (wallet connection, points, airdrops) are layered on as enhancements. Reduce cognitive barriers, increase Web3 feature adoption.
designPrinciples.card2Body: Every feature must answer: "What value does this bring to creators, viewers, and projects respectively?" If it only serves one role, lower its priority. Ensure positive ecosystem circulation.
designPrinciples.card3Body: Use points in the MVP phase to validate user behavior models, avoiding premature token economics that bring compliance and liquidity risks. Data first, reduce decision risk.
designPrinciples.ownershipTag: I led — The three principles were proposed by me after competitive analysis and interviews, then confirmed by team discussion.

productPositioning.label: 03 — PRODUCT POSITIONING
productPositioning.heading: Defining the Business Model & Product Value
productPositioning.body: Positioned the product as a Web3 Chinese-language video platform, serving as a blockchain media hub for the Chinese-speaking market, providing curated, real-time, comprehensive industry information.
productPositioning.calloutBody: After analyzing competitors from each user type's perspective, I converged on three key differentiators: ① Zero content censorship for Web3, creator revenue unaffected by platform policies. ② Native interaction via wallet tasks, viewer engagement depth far exceeding traditional platforms. ③ Precision ad channel reaching active crypto users, higher project ad efficiency.
productPositioning.img1Caption: Competitive advantage comparison by user type

designDecision.label: 09 — DESIGN DECISION
designDecision.heading: How to Keep Users Coming Back to Web3Vid?
designDecision.body: Retention mechanisms are critical to platform survival. I evaluated three approaches across short-term DAU lift, long-term ecosystem health, and MVP feasibility, ultimately choosing a points-based task system as the primary strategy.
designDecision.optionACons:
Insufficient content during cold start, retention inevitably low
Cannot validate quickly in MVP stage
designDecision.optionAVerdict: Rejected — Long-term direction, but not MVP priority
designDecision.optionBPros:
Effectively boosts short-term DAU
Instant positive feedback, maintains activity during cold start
Points data serves as behavioral baseline for future token economics
designDecision.optionBCons: May attract reward farmers (anti-cheat mechanisms needed)
designDecision.optionCCons:
MVP user base too small, social features will feel empty
High dev cost, distracts from core experience
designDecision.optionCVerdict: Rejected — User Base Insufficient to Support
designDecision.calloutBody: B (points task system) as primary, A as long-term direction. The MVP priority is quickly validating whether users will watch videos on this platform. Points provide instant positive feedback, maintaining user activity even when content volume isn't rich enough. Meanwhile, points data serves as a behavioral baseline for future token economics.
designDecision.ownershipTag: I led — The three-option comparison and final decision were proposed and led by me in team discussions.

behavioralDesign.label: 10 — BEHAVIORAL DESIGN
behavioralDesign.heading: Behavioral Psychology-Driven Points Mechanism Design
behavioralDesign.body: After confirming the points task system, I applied behavioral psychology principles to design specific reward mechanisms — ensuring it's not just "having points" but making the points system genuinely change user behavior.
behavioralDesign.card1Before: Based on B.F. Skinner's operant conditioning theory — when behavior is rewarded, users are more likely to repeat it. After completing a task,
behavioralDesign.card1After: to reinforce the "behavior → reward" psychological connection.
behavioralDesign.card2Before: Applying the Goal Gradient Effect — the closer to the goal, the stronger the motivation. Designed a
behavioralDesign.card2After: so users can see how many points remain until the reward — the "almost there" feeling drives the last mile.
behavioralDesign.card3After: keep users returning because they "don't want to break their streak."

featureDesign.label: 11 — FEATURE DESIGN
featureDesign.heading: Merging Web2 & Web3 for a New Feature Experience
featureDesign.body: Combining familiar video watching interfaces with Web3 wallet task functionality. Core design strategy: make Web2 parts "require no learning" while Web3 parts are "worth exploring."
featureDesign.story1After: , I can connect my wallet while watching videos, complete on-chain tasks to earn points, giving my viewing behavior direct returns.
featureDesign.story2After: , I can bind interactive tasks to my videos, deepening viewer engagement while earning project collaboration revenue.
featureDesign.story3After: , I can distribute native ads via wallet tasks, precisely reaching active crypto users who have connected their wallets.

coreFlow.label: 12 — CORE FLOW
coreFlow.heading: Wallet Tasks × Points Lottery: The Platform Core Flow
coreFlow.body: The video platform combined with wallet task lottery flow is Web3Vid's core differentiator. I designed a "Watch → Connect → Complete → Reward" closed-loop flow, letting users naturally encounter Web3 interactions during their viewing experience.
coreFlow.ownershipTag: I led — This core flow was independently designed by me, from User Flow to UI details.
coreFlow.flowCaption: Complete Wallet Task → Points → Lottery User Flow
coreFlow.img1Caption: Wallet connection flow — supports MetaMask, WalletConnect

informationArchitecture.label: 13 — INFORMATION ARCHITECTURE
informationArchitecture.heading: Designing a Web3 Category System — Balancing Browsing & Search
informationArchitecture.body: Web3 has numerous tracks. I designed a dual-layer categorization architecture so users with different goals can quickly find the content they want.
informationArchitecture.text:
Main Categories (Tab level): Trending, Breaking News, Market Analysis, Hot Topics, Tutorials, Airdrop Guides, Deep Dives — helping users immediately find their area of interest.

Sub-categories (Hashtag level): Video hashtags provide more precise topic filtering (e.g., #BTCHalving #Layer2 #DeFi), suitable for users with clear goals.

Category items were determined through Web3 KOL content topic frequency analysis and team industry experience, identifying the top 7 high-frequency themes as main categories.

uiDesign.label: 14 — UI DESIGN
uiDesign.heading: Integrating Research into an MVP That Fits Product Positioning
uiDesign.body: Platform video content includes long-form videos, short videos, and podcast audio. Considering existing user habits, the main viewing flow mirrors YouTube. Core differentiation lies in wallet interactions, points tasks, and Web3-styled lottery design.
uiDesign.img2Caption: Player — Video viewing & wallet task entry

designSystem.label: 15 — DESIGN SYSTEM
designSystem.heading: Building a Design System for Consistency & Development Efficiency
designSystem.body: To ensure rapid MVP iteration doesn't sacrifice visual consistency, I established a Design System early in the design process, covering colors, typography, and component specifications.
designSystem.ownershipTag: I led — Design System was independently built and maintained by me.

pitchDeck.label: 16 — PITCH DECK
pitchDeck.heading: Pitch Deck Design
pitchDeck.body: I was responsible for integrating product positioning, market analysis, and competitive advantages into an investor pitch deck, used for the Hong Kong Cyberport Incubation Programme application — ultimately reaching the finals.

scalability.label: 17 — SCALABILITY
scalability.heading: Scale Challenges & Mitigation Strategies Anticipated During Design
scalability.body: MVP design can't only look at the present. During the design process, I anticipated scale challenges that would emerge as the product grows, and reserved architectural expansion space.
scalability.card1Body: The existing 7 main categories won't suffice, and hashtags will explode. The architecture reserves positions for "custom categories" and "hashtag search," with algorithmic recommendations planned long-term.
scalability.card2Title: Expanding from Chinese to English Markets
scalability.card2Body: UI copy, category names, and content recommendation logic all need i18n. Design System text components use auto-layout, reserving space for multilingual support.
scalability.card3Body: Many users will only farm points without actually watching videos. Points task design includes a "watch duration threshold" (at least 70% completion), rather than counting mere opens.

prototyping.title: 節奏掌控
prototyping.description: 我擅長從內容中找到最合適的節奏，選擇匹配情緒的音樂，並透過剪輯讓敘事與節拍自然融合。讓畫面不只是「順」，而是有呼吸、有層次，能帶著觀眾走完整段故事。

workflow.title: 畫面層次
workflow.description: 透過字卡動態、轉場設計、特效與插畫素材的適度運用，提升畫面的資訊密度與可看性。不是堆疊效果，而是在關鍵時刻加入，讓畫面更有記憶點與節奏變化。

imageGen.title: 美感平衡
imageGen.description: 我相信影音同樣需要被設計。在動態表現與視覺美感之間取得平衡，讓整體畫面風格一致，並與品牌／IP形象保持高度連動。

vibeCoding.title: 封面設計
vibeCoding.description: 擁有多年平面設計背景，能從品牌識別出發，設計具吸引力且具策略性的封面。不只是好看,更能在第一眼抓住注意力並傳達核心訊息。

JS is passionate about Web3 and communicates efficiently, offering detailed UX suggestions that elevate the final product. A designer with both creativity and professionalism!

hero.name: Jae
hero.paragraph1Accent: Seven years of design experience, with the last four focused on product design in the Web3 and Fintech space.
hero.paragraph1After: I've also worked on dating apps, course booking platforms, admin dashboards, and e-commerce sites — building cross-industry design perspective along the way.
hero.paragraph2: I'm also a 5-year active crypto trader. I follow on-chain activity, macroeconomic data, and market sentiment daily — along with broader equity markets.
hero.paragraph2Accent: As a frequent trader myself, I understand the experience from beginner to power user, which allows me to intuitively grasp what users truly care about when designing Web3 products.
hero.paragraph3: I'm passionate about researching AI tools and have integrated them into the dev team's design workflow — from feature planning and writing spec documents, to designing brand-consistent products, maintaining design quality and attention to detail, and completing frontend implementation via Claude Code in collaboration with backend engineers. From spec writing to frontend development,
hero.paragraph3Accent: I can independently deliver the full design-to-assisted-frontend pipeline through Claude Code & Figma MCP.
hero.paragraph3After: I also invest in leveling up my visual design skills — exploring 3D and motion design to bring richer expressiveness to my work.
hero.paragraph4: Feel free to check out my portfolio, or let's chat about product design, AI tools, or investment trading!

values.title: Design Values
values.desc: Over the course of my design career, I've distilled a few core values that serve as the foundation for every design decision I make.
values.card1Desc: Most of my projects were built from scratch, which means hands-on involvement in product strategy and feature planning. I tend to assess whether a feature should be built before jumping into design.
values.card2Title: Finding the Best Solution Within Constraints
values.card2Desc: Every product has technical and business constraints. I work within the intersection of engineering feasibility, business goals, and user experience to find solutions that can actually ship.
values.card3Desc: Translating complex information architecture and workflows into interfaces users can operate intuitively. Efficient for power users, approachable for newcomers.
values.card4Desc: I stay on top of industry trends and market dynamics, which helps me contribute informed suggestions during feature planning — not just 'this is more usable,' but 'the market needs this right now.'

skills.title: Soft Skills
skills.desc: Beyond solid hard skills, I value communication, collaboration, and continuous learning — traits that keep projects moving smoothly.
skills.card1Desc: I use flow charts, fund flow diagrams, and design specs to translate design intent into language that engineers, PMs, and compliance teams can all understand.
skills.card2Desc: Proactively integrating AI into my workflow: Figma MCP bridging design and dev environments, Claude Code for building side projects. Continuously exploring how AI can meaningfully change how designers work.
skills.card3Desc: Regularly attending PM book clubs, design communities, and AI talks — understanding product development from product, engineering, and business angles. Learning from others' workflows to keep improving my own process and output quality.
